iterations.text:
The redistribution models confirm the asymmetric prediction from §III; they do so precisely where the symmetric account most needed them to fail. RTI predicts slightly higher redistribution support across all regimes (β=0.041, p<0.001). The cross-regime variation driving the exclusion finding does not emerge on the solidarity side. The RTI × Liberal coefficient is small and non-significant (β=0.013, SE=0.019, p=0.488). Welfare institutional context clearly attenuates how vulnerability converts into exclusion. It does not detectably moderate how the same vulnerability converts into redistributive solidarity. This is the asymmetry. It amounts to more than a missing finding; it matches what the theory predicted in §III.F (P2). The symmetric buffering account would need to deny this pattern.

The ISSP 2006 Role of Government module (N=10,216, 12 countries) offers a second test. This test uses a different sample, outcome, and time period. We find no interaction between automation exposure and welfare generosity on unemployment spending support (β=+0.010, SE=0.016, p=0.55). This constitutes a second null. The 2006 data predate the automation discourse of Frey and Osborne (2017) by several years. On our reading, the protective pathway requires automation exposure to register as a salient class-level threat in public discourse. Only then can solidarity be politically organised around it. The damage pathway carries no such contextual requirement. The asymmetry remains detectable across temporal contexts; the underlying material vulnerabilities themselves shift during this period.

A measurement-problem reading of the redistribution null remains available. Our data permit a more precise statement than the bare null alone supports. A two-one-sided-t (TOST) equivalence test against a smallest effect size of interest matched to the exclusion-side moderation magnitude (|β|=0.06, the value the symmetric account would have predicted) returns p_TOST = 0.008 for the ESS redistribution interaction. It returns p_TOST = 0.001 for the ISSP supplementary test (both at α=0.05). The 90% CIs fall entirely within ±0.05. The redistribution null does not merely fail to detect an effect; it positively rules out a moderation of the magnitude the symmetric account would predict. A small protective effect (|β|<0.03) remains within our data's resolution. The symmetric prediction is rejected.

Importantly, the redistribution models confirm the asymmetric prediction set out in §III. They do so precisely where the symmetric account most needed them to fail. RTI predicts slightly more redistribution support across all regimes (β=0.041, p<0.001). The cross-regime variation driving the exclusion finding simply does not appear on the solidarity side; the RTI × Liberal coefficient is small and non-significant (β=0.013, SE=0.019, p=0.488). Welfare institutional context cleanly attenuates how vulnerability converts into exclusion. Yet it does not detectably moderate the conversion of that same vulnerability into redistributive solidarity. This is the asymmetry. It amounts to more than the absence of a finding; it is what the theory predicted in §III.F (P2), and what the symmetric buffering account would have to deny.

The ISSP 2006 Role of Government module (N=10,216, 12 countries) supplies a second test on a different sample, outcome, and time period. No interaction between automation exposure and welfare generosity on unemployment spending support emerges (β=+0.010, SE=0.016, p=0.55). Another null. The 2006 data predate the automation discourse of Frey and Osborne (2017) by several years. On my reading, the protective pathway requires automation exposure to register as a salient class-level threat in public discourse before solidarity can be politically organised around it; the damage pathway carries no such contextual requirement. The asymmetry remains detectable across temporal contexts where the underlying material vulnerabilities are themselves shifting.

A measurement-problem reading of the redistribution null is available. The data permit a more precise statement than the bare null itself supports. A two-one-sided-t (TOST) equivalence test against a smallest-effect-size-of-interest matched to the exclusion-side moderation magnitude (|β|=0.06, the value the symmetric account would have predicted) returns p_TOST = 0.008 for the ESS redistribution interaction and p_TOST = 0.001 for the ISSP supplementary test (both at α=0.05). The 90% CIs lie entirely within ±0.05. This matters. The redistribution null does not merely fail to detect an effect; it positively rules out a moderation of the magnitude the symmetric account would predict. A small protective effect (|β|<0.03) remains within the data's resolution, but the symmetric prediction is rejected.

The redistribution models confirm the asymmetric prediction set out in §III; they do so precisely where the symmetric account most needed them to fail. RTI predicts slightly more redistribution support across all regimes (β=0.041, p<0.001). We observe something telling here. The cross-regime variation driving the exclusion finding? It simply does not appear on the solidarity side. The RTI × Liberal coefficient is small and non-significant (β=0.013, SE=0.019, p=0.488). Welfare institutional context cleanly attenuates how vulnerability converts into exclusion; it does not detectably moderate that same vulnerability's conversion into redistributive solidarity. That is the asymmetry. Not merely an absent finding. This is what the theory predicted in §III.F (P2); it is what the symmetric buffering account would have to deny.

We turn now to replication. The ISSP 2006 Role of Government module (N=10,216, 12 countries) provides a second test on a different sample, outcome, and time period. The result: no interaction between automation exposure and welfare generosity on unemployment spending support (β=+0.010, SE=0.016, p=0.55). A second null. Consider the temporal context. The 2006 data predate the automation discourse of Frey and Osborne (2017) by several years. Our reading is this: the protective pathway requires automation exposure to register as a salient class-level threat in public discourse before solidarity can be politically organised around it; the damage pathway carries no such contextual requirement. The asymmetry is detectable across temporal contexts where underlying material vulnerabilities are themselves shifting.

A measurement-problem reading of the redistribution null is available. We can be more precise than the bare null itself supports. A two-one-sided-t (TOST) equivalence test against a smallest-effect-size-of-interest matched to the exclusion-side moderation magnitude (|β|=0.06, the value the symmetric account would have predicted) returns p_TOST = 0.008 for the ESS redistribution interaction and p_TOST = 0.001 for the ISSP supplementary test (both at α=0.05). The 90% CIs lie entirely within ±0.05. What does this mean? The redistribution null does not merely fail to detect an effect; it positively rules out a moderation of the magnitude the symmetric account would predict. A small protective effect (|β|<0.03) remains within our data's resolution. But the symmetric prediction is rejected.
